topic_title: Writing to a file — open, write, close
verdict: PASS — all checks clean. Format discipline met (no frontmatter, six sections in order). Word count 1350 within [1200,1600] target. All three citations used at least once with no fabricated entries. No forward references detected. Beginner readability is strong: all new terms defined on first use with bold-term pattern, procedures use numbered steps, paragraphs short. No scope creep. All corpus-traced assertions verified. All 4 corpus-critic soft findings resolved: list comprehension replaced with plain for-loop (lines 84–91), tuple syntax replaced with parallel lists throughout Worked Example (lines 127–190), repr() removed and replaced with print(line, end='') (lines 154–162), docstring replaced with plain comment (line 171).

notes: str() — referenced to Week 11, which is a prior week. PASS.
with block, file object, open() — referenced to Topic 12.6. PASS.
for loop, list — referenced to Topic 12.4. PASS.
def, functions — referenced to Topics 12.1–12.3. PASS.

detail.notes: All new terms (write mode, append mode, .write(), .writelines(), buffer/flush) defined at first use with bold-term pattern. Concepts introduced with examples/analogies before abstraction. Plain active register throughout. Average sentence length well under 25 words.
Procedures use numbered steps. All paragraphs ≤4 sentences. Inline code examples present in every Key Concepts subsection. Definitions use bold-term / plain-meaning shape consistently.

assessment_references.notes: No assessment IDs, due weeks, or grade weights appear in the reading.

scope.notes: Content bounded to write mode, append mode, .write(), .writelines(), with block, str() conversion — all on-headline. import datetime mentioned only in In Practice section with explicit deferral ('full datetime API is a later topic'). No scope creep detected.

sampled_assertions.assertion: Write mode erases existing content before writing
Append mode does not erase; jumps to end
sampled_assertions.corpus_support: §5.2 — 'Python opens it without erasing anything. The internal bookmark jumps straight to the very end'

sampled_assertions.assertion: .writelines() does not add newlines between items
with block guarantees close and flush even on error
sampled_assertions.corpus_support: §5.6 — 'Guaranteed close. If something goes wrong...Python still closes the file'
§5.7 — 'f.write(score) # TypeError: write() argument must be str, not int'

sampled_assertions.assertion: Opening in write mode inside a loop re-erases on every iteration
sampled_assertions.corpus_support: §8 Best Practices #2 — 'opens in write mode on every iteration, erasing previous output'

sampled_assertions.assertion: str() converts any value to string representation
sampled_assertions.corpus_support: §5.7 — 'str() is the function that converts any value to its string representation'

detail.list_comprehension: Corpus §5.5 had [s + '\n' for s in raw]. Reading lines 84–91 replace this with a plain for-loop using lines.append(s + '\n'). RESOLVED.
detail.tuple_syntax: Corpus §6 used students = [("Alice", 88), ...] and for name, score in students. Reading Worked Example uses parallel lists names/scores with for i in range(len(names)) indexing throughout. RESOLVED.
detail.repr_removed: Corpus §6.2 had print(repr(line)). Reading Step 2 (lines 154–162) replaces with print(line, end='') — no repr() call present. RESOLVED.
detail.docstring_handled: Corpus §6.3 had triple-quoted docstrings. Reading Step 3 (line 171) uses a plain # comment: '# This function saves names and scores to the given file'. RESOLVED.
